name: Tuatha De Danann
shortDescription: The Tuatha Dé Danann are a supernatural race in Irish mythology. They are believed to be the principal deities of pre-Christian Ireland.
description: The Tuatha Dé Danann (meaning "People of the Goddess Danu") represent the main pantheon of deities in Irish mythology. They are depicted as a supernatural race possessing magical skills and associated with the pre-Christian inhabitants of Ireland. According to legend, they arrived in Ireland from the north or from islands in the west, often veiled in mist and possessing advanced knowledge and craftsmanship. The Tuatha Dé Danann waged war against the Fir Bolg for control of Ireland, eventually winning at the Battle of Moytura. They later faced the Fomorians, monstrous beings representing chaos and destruction, in a second, more devastating Battle of Moytura. Lugh, a prominent figure among the Tuatha Dé Danann, played a crucial role in defeating the Fomorians and securing their victory. After their defeat by the Milesians (the ancestors of the Irish people), the Tuatha Dé Danann retreated into the sídhe (fairy mounds), becoming the aos sí (fairy folk). They are often associated with beauty, magic, skill, and wisdom, and continue to feature in Irish folklore and literature. Figures such as Dagda, a father-figure and powerful leader, Lugh, a skilled warrior and craftsman, and Brigid, a goddess of poetry, healing, and smithcraft, are prominent members of the Tuatha Dé Danann. Their stories explore themes of power, fate, and the transition from pagan beliefs to Christianity.
